DAT675 – Project¶

Under What Biological and Statistical Conditions Does Multi-Task Learning Help or Hurt Toxicity Prediction?¶

Team Members
Kevin Cheng (kevinche@chalmers.se)
Qiuge Song (qiuge@chalmers.se)

Project Overview¶

This project investigates when multi-task learning (MTL) improves toxicity prediction and when it instead leads to negative transfer, compared with single-task learning (STL).

Using the Tox21 dataset, we compare endpoint-level performance across classical machine learning baselines and graph neural network models. Our goal is not only to determine whether MTL performs better, but also to understand under what biological and statistical conditions parameter sharing becomes helpful or harmful.

More specifically, we study whether transfer behavior can be explained by:

  • biological grouping, especially the difference between Nuclear Receptor (NR) and Stress Response (SR) tasks,
  • statistical properties of the dataset, including label coverage, class imbalance, and inter-task label correlation,
  • and the final transfer outcome, measured as improvement or degradation of MTL relative to STL.

Revised Project Scope¶

To keep the project focused and feasible, we define the scope as follows:

  1. One dataset: Tox21
  2. One main evaluation protocol: scaffold split
  3. One main deep model family: GCN
  4. Two classical baselines: Logistic Regression and Random Forest
  5. One main comparison: STL vs MTL at the endpoint level

Working Definitions¶

  • Biological similarity: endpoints belonging to the same Tox21 super-group (NR or SR), supported by label-correlation analysis
  • Negative transfer: endpoint-level test AUPRC for MTL is lower than that for STL
  • Gradient analysis: treated as an optional extension after stable STL and MTL baselines are established

Summary of the Pipeline¶

Overall, the notebook follows the workflow below:

Data profiling → Scaffold-based splitting → Feature engineering → Baseline modeling → Graph modeling → Repeated evaluation → Transfer analysis → Visualization → Final reporting

This structure is designed to keep the project clear, reproducible, and easy to follow from start to finish.

1. Data Profiling and Preprocessing¶

This section prepares the dataset and ensures data quality before modeling. It focuses on understanding data characteristics and removing inconsistencies that could affect downstream results.

1.1 Imports and Global Settings¶

Set up the environment, random seeds, and global variables to ensure reproducibility and consistent configuration across the pipeline.

In [4]:
# ============================================================
# 1.1 Imports and Global Settings
# ============================================================

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import MolStandardize
from rdkit.Chem.Scaffolds import MurckoScaffold
from sklearn.metrics import matthews_corrcoef

# ----------------------------
# Global settings
# ----------------------------
possible_paths = [
    Path("../data/raw/tox21.csv"),   # if notebook runs inside /notebooks
    Path("data/raw/tox21.csv"),      # if notebook runs from repo root
    Path("tox21.csv")                # fallback
]

DATA_PATH = None
for p in possible_paths:
    if p.exists():
        DATA_PATH = p.resolve()
        break

assert DATA_PATH is not None, "Data file not found in expected locations"

SMILES_COL = "smiles"

ALL_ENDPOINTS = [
    "NR-AR", "NR-AR-LBD", "NR-AhR", "NR-Aromatase", "NR-ER", "NR-ER-LBD",
    "NR-PPAR-gamma",
    "SR-ARE", "SR-ATAD5", "SR-HSE", "SR-MMP", "SR-p53"
]

NR_ENDPOINTS = [
    "NR-AR", "NR-AR-LBD", "NR-AhR", "NR-Aromatase", "NR-ER", "NR-ER-LBD",
    "NR-PPAR-gamma"
]

SR_ENDPOINTS = [
    "SR-ARE", "SR-ATAD5", "SR-HSE", "SR-MMP", "SR-p53"
]

SEED = 42
np.random.seed(SEED)

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 100)
pd.set_option("display.width", 140)

print("Imports and global settings ready.")
print("Using data path:", DATA_PATH)
print("Number of endpoints:", len(ALL_ENDPOINTS))

Imports and global settings ready.
Using data path: C:\Users\lindy\Downloads\tox21.csv
Number of endpoints: 12

1.2 Dataset Loading and Initial Inspection¶

Load the Tox21 dataset and inspect its structure (size, columns, missing values) to understand the overall data distribution.

In [5]:
# ============================================================
# 1.2 Dataset Loading and Initial Inspection
# ============================================================

assert DATA_PATH.exists(), f"Data file not found: {DATA_PATH}"

df_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH)
df = df_raw.copy()

assert SMILES_COL in df.columns, f"Missing required SMILES column: {SMILES_COL}"

missing_endpoints = [c for c in ALL_ENDPOINTS if c not in df.columns]
assert len(missing_endpoints) == 0, f"Missing endpoint columns: {missing_endpoints}"

initial_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item": [
        "Number of molecules",
        "Number of columns",
        "SMILES missing",
        "Duplicate SMILES",
        "Number of endpoints"
    ],
    "Value": [
        len(df),
        df.shape[1],
        int(df[SMILES_COL].isna().sum()),
        int(df[SMILES_COL].duplicated().sum()),
        len(ALL_ENDPOINTS)
    ]
})

print(f"Raw dataset shape: {df.shape}")
display(initial_summary)

Raw dataset shape: (7831, 14)
Item Value
0 Number of molecules 7831
1 Number of columns 14
2 SMILES missing 0
3 Duplicate SMILES 0
4 Number of endpoints 12

1.3 Data Cleaning and SMILES Standardization¶

Standardize SMILES strings and remove invalid or duplicate molecules. This ensures that molecular representations are consistent and comparable across the dataset.

In [3]:
# ============================================================
# 1.3 Data Cleaning and SMILES Standardization
# ============================================================
from rdkit import RDLogger
RDLogger.DisableLog('rdApp.*')
# RDKit standardization tools
normalizer = MolStandardize.rdMolStandardize.Normalizer()
reionizer = MolStandardize.rdMolStandardize.Reionizer()
uncharger = MolStandardize.rdMolStandardize.Uncharger()
fragment_chooser = MolStandardize.rdMolStandardize.LargestFragmentChooser()

def standardize_smiles(smiles):
    if pd.isna(smiles):
        return None
    
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return None
    
    try:
        mol = fragment_chooser.choose(mol)
        mol = normalizer.normalize(mol)
        mol = reionizer.reionize(mol)
        mol = uncharger.uncharge(mol)
        return Chem.MolToSmiles(mol, canonical=True)
    except Exception:
        return None

# ----------------------------
# Cleaning pipeline (silent)
# ----------------------------
n_before = len(df)

df = df.reset_index(drop=False).rename(columns={"index": "orig_idx"})
df["CanonicalSMILES"] = df[SMILES_COL].apply(standardize_smiles)

n_invalid = int(df["CanonicalSMILES"].isna().sum())

df = df.loc[df["CanonicalSMILES"].notna()].copy()

n_before_dedup = len(df)
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset="CanonicalSMILES").reset_index(drop=True)
n_removed_dup = n_before_dedup - len(df)

# ----------------------------
# Clean summary (ONLY output)
# ----------------------------
cleaning_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Raw molecules",
        "Invalid SMILES removed",
        "Duplicates removed",
        "Final dataset size"
    ],
    "Value": [
        n_before,
        n_invalid,
        n_removed_dup,
        len(df)
    ]
})

display(cleaning_summary)
Metric Value
0 Raw molecules 7831
1 Invalid SMILES removed 8
2 Duplicates removed 210
3 Final dataset size 7613

1.4 Missing Label Analysis¶

Analyze label sparsity across endpoints to understand data availability. This is critical because missing labels directly affect training and loss computation.

In [4]:
# ============================================================
# 1.4 Missing Label Analysis
# ============================================================

missing_rows = []
n_total = len(df)

for endpoint in ALL_ENDPOINTS:
    n_missing = int(df[endpoint].isna().sum())
    n_observed = n_total - n_missing
    coverage = n_observed / n_total
    
    missing_rows.append({
        "Endpoint": endpoint,
        "Observed labels": n_observed,
        "Missing labels": n_missing,
        "Coverage": round(coverage, 3)
    })

missing_df = pd.DataFrame(missing_rows).sort_values(
    by=["Coverage", "Endpoint"], ascending=[True, True]
).reset_index(drop=True)

display(missing_df)

print("Mean label coverage:", round(missing_df["Coverage"].mean(), 3))
print("Lowest coverage endpoint:", missing_df.iloc[0]["Endpoint"])
print("Highest coverage endpoint:", missing_df.iloc[-1]["Endpoint"])
Endpoint Observed labels Missing labels Coverage
0 NR-Aromatase 5629 1984 0.739
1 SR-MMP 5636 1977 0.740
2 SR-ARE 5664 1949 0.744
3 NR-ER 6019 1594 0.791
4 NR-PPAR-gamma 6261 1352 0.822
5 SR-HSE 6284 1329 0.825
6 NR-AhR 6354 1259 0.835
7 NR-AR-LBD 6559 1054 0.862
8 SR-p53 6582 1031 0.865
9 NR-ER-LBD 6759 854 0.888
10 SR-ATAD5 6867 746 0.902
11 NR-AR 7050 563 0.926 
Mean label coverage: 0.828
Lowest coverage endpoint: NR-Aromatase
Highest coverage endpoint: NR-AR

Analysis of Label Coverage¶

The Tox21 dataset has relatively good label coverage overall, with a mean coverage of 0.828. This means that most endpoints are labeled for a large proportion of molecules. However, the coverage is not completely uniform across tasks.

For example, NR-Aromatase has the lowest coverage (0.739), while NR-AR has the highest coverage (0.926). This difference shows that some endpoints provide less training information than others. As a result, endpoints with lower coverage may be harder to learn and may produce less stable evaluation results.

Overall, label sparsity is present, but it is not extreme. It is still an important factor to consider later when comparing STL and MTL performance.

1.5 Class Imbalance Analysis¶

Analyze label sparsity across endpoints to understand data availability. This is critical because missing labels directly affect training and loss computation.

In [6]:
# ============================================================
# 1.5 Class Imbalance Analysis
# ============================================================

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ----------------------------
# Compute imbalance table
# ----------------------------
imbalance_rows = []

for endpoint in ALL_ENDPOINTS:
    y = df[endpoint]

    n_obs = y.notna().sum()
    n_pos = (y == 1).sum()

    pos_rate = n_pos / n_obs if n_obs > 0 else np.nan

    imbalance_rows.append({
        "Endpoint": endpoint,
        "Observed labels": n_obs,
        "Positive rate": pos_rate
    })

imbalance_df = pd.DataFrame(imbalance_rows)

# ----------------------------
# Convert to percentage
# ----------------------------
plot_df = imbalance_df.copy()
plot_df["Pos Rate (%)"] = plot_df["Positive rate"] * 100

# ----------------------------
# Categorize imbalance
# ----------------------------
def imbalance_category(p):
    if p < 5:
        return "Severe (<5%)"
    elif p < 10:
        return "High (5–10%)"
    elif p < 20:
        return "Moderate (10–20%)"
    else:
        return "Relatively balanced (>20%)"

plot_df["Imbalance"] = plot_df["Pos Rate (%)"].apply(imbalance_category)

# ----------------------------
# Plot
# ----------------------------
palette = {
    "Severe (<5%)": "#C44E52",
    "High (5–10%)": "#DD8452",
    "Moderate (10–20%)": "#4C72B0",
    "Relatively balanced (>20%)": "#55A868"
}

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4.8))

sns.barplot(
    data=plot_df.sort_values("Pos Rate (%)"),
    x="Endpoint",
    y="Pos Rate (%)",
    hue="Imbalance",
    dodge=False,
    palette=palette
)

plt.axhline(5, linestyle="--", color="black", linewidth=1)

plt.xticks(rotation=40, ha="right")
plt.ylabel("Positive Rate (%)")
plt.xlabel("")
plt.title("Class Imbalance Across Tox21 Endpoints", fontsize=13, weight="bold")

plt.legend(title="Imbalance Level")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ----------------------------
# Clean summary table
# ----------------------------
display(
    imbalance_df.style.format({
        "Positive rate": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Endpoint Observed labels Positive rate
0 NR-AR 7050 0.043
1 NR-AR-LBD 6559 0.036
2 NR-AhR 6354 0.119
3 NR-Aromatase 5629 0.050
4 NR-ER 6019 0.129
5 NR-ER-LBD 6759 0.051
6 NR-PPAR-gamma 6261 0.029
7 SR-ARE 5664 0.161
8 SR-ATAD5 6867 0.038
9 SR-HSE 6284 0.057
10 SR-MMP 5636 0.159
11 SR-p53 6582 0.063

Analysis of Class Imbalance¶

Although label coverage is fairly high, the dataset shows clear class imbalance across all endpoints. The positive class is rare in every task, and several endpoints fall below the 5% positive rate threshold, which indicates severe imbalance.

This is important because imbalanced labels make toxicity prediction more difficult. A model can easily become biased toward the negative class, especially for endpoints with very few positive samples. For this reason, metrics such as AUPRC are more informative than simple accuracy.

The imbalance level also varies across endpoints. For example, NR-PPAR-gamma and SR-ATAD5 are among the most imbalanced tasks, while SR-ARE and SR-MMP have relatively higher positive rates. This suggests that some endpoints may be easier to learn than others, which may later influence transfer behavior in multi-task learning.

1.6 Bemis–Murcko Scaffold Generation¶

Generate scaffolds to represent core molecular structures. This enables structure-based splitting, which better reflects real-world generalization.

In [7]:
# ============================================================
# 1.6 Bemis–Murcko Scaffold Generation
# ============================================================

def get_scaffold(canonical_smiles):
    if pd.isna(canonical_smiles):
        return None
    
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(canonical_smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return None
    
    try:
        scaffold = MurckoScaffold.MurckoScaffoldSmiles(mol=mol)
        if scaffold == "":
            return None
        return scaffold
    except Exception:
        return None

df["MurckoScaffold"] = df["CanonicalSMILES"].apply(get_scaffold)
df["EmptyScaffold"] = df["MurckoScaffold"].isna()

# For molecules without Murcko scaffold, use canonical SMILES as fallback scaffold
df["Scaffold"] = df["MurckoScaffold"].fillna(df["CanonicalSMILES"])

scaffold_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Total molecules",
        "Unique canonical SMILES",
        "Unique Murcko scaffolds (excluding empty)",
        "Empty Murcko scaffolds",
        "Final missing Scaffold values",
        "Unique final scaffold groups"
    ],
    "Value": [
        len(df),
        df["CanonicalSMILES"].nunique(),
        df["MurckoScaffold"].dropna().nunique(),
        int(df["EmptyScaffold"].sum()),
        int(df["Scaffold"].isna().sum()),
        df["Scaffold"].nunique()
    ]
})

display(scaffold_summary)
display(df[[SMILES_COL, "CanonicalSMILES", "MurckoScaffold", "EmptyScaffold", "Scaffold"]].head(5))
Metric Value

1 Unique canonical SMILES 7613
2 Unique Murcko scaffolds (excluding empty) 2272
3 Empty Murcko scaffolds 1716
4 Final missing Scaffold values 0
5 Unique final scaffold groups 3988
smiles CanonicalSMILES MurckoScaffold EmptyScaffold Scaffold

2 CC[C@]1(O)CC[C@H]2[C@@H]3CCC4=CCCC[C@@H]4[C@H]... CC[C@]1(O)CC[C@H]2[C@@H]3CCC4=CCCC[C@@H]4[C@H]... C1=C2CCC3C4CCCC4CCC3C2CCC1 False C1=C2CCC3C4CCCC4CCC3C2CCC1
