4 CC(O)(P(=O)(O)O)P(=O)(O)O CC(O)(P(=O)(O)O)P(=O)(O)O None True CC(O)(P(=O)(O)O)P(=O)(O)O

1.7 Phi correlation heatmap¶

Generate scaffolds to represent core molecular structures. This enables structure-based splitting, which better reflects real-world generalization.

In [8]:
# ============================================================
# Phi correlation heatmap 
# ============================================================

import seaborn as sns  

task_order = NR_ENDPOINTS + SR_ENDPOINTS
label_matrix = df[task_order].copy()

# -------------------------
# Compute Phi correlation
# -------------------------
n_tasks = len(task_order)
phi_matrix = np.full((n_tasks, n_tasks), np.nan)

for i in range(n_tasks):
    for j in range(n_tasks):
        mask = label_matrix[[task_order[i], task_order[j]]].notna().all(axis=1)

        if mask.sum() < 10:
            continue

        a = label_matrix.loc[mask, task_order[i]].values
        b = label_matrix.loc[mask, task_order[j]].values

        if a.std() == 0 or b.std() == 0:
            phi_matrix[i, j] = 0.0
        else:
            phi_matrix[i, j] = np.corrcoef(a, b)[0, 1]

phi_df = pd.DataFrame(phi_matrix, index=task_order, columns=task_order)

# -------------------------
# Plot heatmap
# -------------------------
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

sns.heatmap(
    phi_df,
    annot=True,
    fmt=".2f",
    cmap="RdBu_r",
    center=0,
    vmin=-0.4,   
    vmax=0.4,
    square=True,
    linewidths=0.5,
    linecolor="white",
    cbar_kws={"label": "Phi coefficient"},
    annot_kws={"size": 7},
    ax=ax
)

# -------------------------
# Draw NR / SR blocks
# -------------------------
n_nr = len(NR_ENDPOINTS)
n_sr = len(SR_ENDPOINTS)

# NR block
ax.add_patch(
    plt.Rectangle((0, 0), n_nr, n_nr, fill=False, edgecolor="#3B5B92", lw=2.5)
)

# SR block
ax.add_patch(
    plt.Rectangle((n_nr, n_nr), n_sr, n_sr, fill=False, edgecolor="#B04A3A", lw=2.5)
)

# -------------------------
# Labels and title
# -------------------------
ax.set_title(
    "Inter-task Phi Correlation Matrix",
    fontsize=14,
    weight="bold",
    pad=12
)

ax.set_xticklabels(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha="right", fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticklabels(ax.get_yticklabels(), rotation=0, fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [9]:
# ============================================================
# Phi Summary Table (Clean + Robust)
# ============================================================

def summarize_phi(values):
    if len(values) == 0:
        return [np.nan] * 4 + [0]
    return [
        np.mean(values),
        np.median(values),
        np.min(values),
        np.max(values),
        len(values)
    ]

within_nr = []
within_sr = []
cross_nr_sr = []

for i, e1 in enumerate(ALL_ENDPOINTS):
    for j, e2 in enumerate(ALL_ENDPOINTS):
        if j <= i:
            continue

        idx1 = ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e1)
        idx2 = ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e2)
        val = phi_matrix[idx1, idx2]
        if pd.isna(val):
            continue

        if (e1 in NR_ENDPOINTS) and (e2 in NR_ENDPOINTS):
            within_nr.append(val)
        elif (e1 in SR_ENDPOINTS) and (e2 in SR_ENDPOINTS):
            within_sr.append(val)
        else:
            cross_nr_sr.append(val)

phi_summary = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        ["Within NR", *summarize_phi(within_nr)],
        ["Within SR", *summarize_phi(within_sr)],
        ["Cross NR-SR", *summarize_phi(cross_nr_sr)]
    ],
    columns=[
        "Group",
        "Mean Phi",
        "Median Phi",
        "Min Phi",
        "Max Phi",
        "Number of pairs"
    ]
)

# Sort groups for nicer reading (optional but clean)
phi_summary = phi_summary.set_index("Group")

display(
    phi_summary.style
    .format({
        "Mean Phi": "{:.3f}",
        "Median Phi": "{:.3f}",
        "Min Phi": "{:.3f}",
        "Max Phi": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Mean Phi Median Phi Min Phi Max Phi Number of pairs
Group          
Within NR 0.168 0.127 -0.018 0.572 21
Within SR 0.305 0.302 0.181 0.439 10
Cross NR-SR 0.190 0.200 -0.020 0.350 35

Analysis of Inter-Task Phi Correlation¶

The Phi correlation matrix shows that task similarity is not uniform across Tox21 endpoints. Some endpoint pairs have relatively strong positive correlations, while others are only weakly related.

Within the NR group, a few pairs such as NR-AR / NR-AR-LBD and NR-ER / NR-ER-LBD show clearly stronger correlations. This makes sense because these endpoints are biologically related and may share similar response patterns. Within the SR group, correlations are also generally positive, with several moderate relationships among endpoints such as SR-ARE, SR-MMP, and SR-p53.

At the same time, cross-group correlations between NR and SR are not consistently low. Some NR–SR pairs still show moderate positive correlation. This suggests that biological grouping alone does not fully explain task similarity.

Overall, the Phi matrix suggests that multi-task learning may help when tasks share stronger label structure, but the benefit is likely to depend on the specific endpoint combination rather than only on the NR/SR grouping.

In [10]:
# ============================================================
# Sanity Check Before Section 2
# ============================================================

required_cols = ["orig_idx", SMILES_COL, "CanonicalSMILES", "Scaffold"] + ALL_ENDPOINTS
missing_required = [c for c in required_cols if c not in df.columns]

assert len(missing_required) == 0, f"Missing required columns before Section 2: {missing_required}"

print("Section 1 completed successfully.")
print("Current dataframe shape:", df.shape)
print("Ready for scaffold-based splitting.")

Section 1 completed successfully.
Current dataframe shape: (7613, 19)
Ready for scaffold-based splitting.

2. Scaffold-Based Data Splitting¶

This section defines a realistic and robust evaluation protocol based on molecular structure rather than random sampling.

2.1 Grouping Molecules by Scaffold¶

This section defines a realistic and robust evaluation protocol based on molecular structure rather than random sampling.

In [11]:
# ============================================================
# 2.1 Grouping Molecules by Scaffold
# ============================================================

from collections import defaultdict
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

assert "Scaffold" in df.columns, "Column 'Scaffold' not found. Please run Section 1.6 first."

# Reset index to make row tracking stable
df = df.reset_index(drop=True).copy()

# Group row indices by scaffold
scaffold_to_indices = defaultdict(list)
for idx, scaffold in enumerate(df["Scaffold"]):
    scaffold_to_indices[scaffold].append(idx)

# Sort scaffold groups by size (largest first)
scaffold_groups = sorted(scaffold_to_indices.values(), key=len, reverse=True)

scaffold_group_sizes = [len(g) for g in scaffold_groups]

grouping_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Total molecules",
        "Unique scaffold groups",
        "Largest scaffold group",
        "Median scaffold group size",
        "Singleton scaffold groups"
    ],
    "Value": [
        len(df),
        len(scaffold_groups),
        max(scaffold_group_sizes),
        int(np.median(scaffold_group_sizes)),
        sum(1 for x in scaffold_group_sizes if x == 1)
    ]
})

display(grouping_summary)
Metric Value
0 Total molecules 7613
1 Unique scaffold groups 3988
2 Largest scaffold group 1456
3 Median scaffold group size 1
4 Singleton scaffold groups 3438

2.2 Generating Repeated Scaffold Splits¶

Create multiple scaffold-based splits to improve robustness and reduce dependence on a single partition.

In [12]:
# ============================================================
# 2.2 Generating Repeated Scaffold Splits
# ============================================================

def make_scaffold_split(df, scaffold_groups, seed=42, train_frac=0.8, val_frac=0.1, test_frac=0.1):
    """
    Assign scaffold groups to train / val / test without scaffold overlap.
    """
    assert abs(train_frac + val_frac + test_frac - 1.0) < 1e-8

    rng = np.random.RandomState(seed)
    groups = scaffold_groups.copy()
    rng.shuffle(groups)

    n_total = len(df)
    train_target = int(round(train_frac * n_total))
    val_target = int(round(val_frac * n_total))

    train_idx, val_idx, test_idx = [], [], []

    for group in groups:
        if len(train_idx) + len(group) <= train_target:
            train_idx.extend(group)
        elif len(val_idx) + len(group) <= val_target:
            val_idx.extend(group)
        else:
            test_idx.extend(group)

    # If val set ends up too small due to scaffold granularity, rebalance from test
    if len(val_idx) == 0 and len(test_idx) > 0:
        moved_group = test_idx[: max(1, len(test_idx) // 2)]
        val_idx.extend(moved_group)
        test_idx = test_idx[len(moved_group):]

    return {
        "train_idx": sorted(train_idx),
        "val_idx": sorted(val_idx),
        "test_idx": sorted(test_idx)
    }


N_SPLITS = 5
split_packs = []

for split_id in range(N_SPLITS):
    split_seed = SEED + split_id
    split = make_scaffold_split(
        df=df,
        scaffold_groups=scaffold_groups,
        seed=split_seed,
        train_frac=0.8,
        val_frac=0.1,
        test_frac=0.1
    )
    split["split_id"] = split_id
    split_packs.append(split)

2.3 Train / Validation / Test Partitioning¶

Divide scaffold groups into train, validation, and test sets (80/10/10) to ensure proper model development and evaluation.

In [13]:
# ============================================================
# 2.3 Train / Validation / Test Partitioning
# ============================================================

def build_split_dataframes(df, split):
    train_df = df.loc[split["train_idx"]].copy()
    val_df   = df.loc[split["val_idx"]].copy()
    test_df  = df.loc[split["test_idx"]].copy()

    return {
        "train": train_df.reset_index(drop=True),
        "val": val_df.reset_index(drop=True),
        "test": test_df.reset_index(drop=True)
    }

# Build dataframe versions for all splits
split_data = [build_split_dataframes(df, split) for split in split_packs]

# Summary table across splits
split_summary_rows = []
for split, split_pack in zip(split_packs, split_data):
    n_train = len(split_pack["train"])
    n_val   = len(split_pack["val"])
    n_test  = len(split_pack["test"])
    n_total = n_train + n_val + n_test

    split_summary_rows.append({
        "Split": split["split_id"],
        "Train": n_train,
        "Validation": n_val,
        "Test": n_test,
        "Train frac": n_train / n_total,
        "Val frac": n_val / n_total,
        "Test frac": n_test / n_total
    })

split_summary_df = pd.DataFrame(split_summary_rows)

display(
    split_summary_df.style.format({
        "Train frac": "{:.3f}",
        "Val frac": "{:.3f}",
        "Test frac": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Split Train Validation Test Train frac Val frac Test frac
0 0 6090 761 762 0.800 0.100 0.100
1 1 6090 761 762 0.800 0.100 0.100
2 2 6090 761 762 0.800 0.100 0.100
3 3 6090 761 762 0.800 0.100 0.100
4 4 6090 761 762 0.800 0.100 0.100

The scaffold-based splitting is very consistent across all five splits. In every case, the train, validation, and test sets follow the intended 80/10/10 ratio exactly.

This shows that scaffold grouping strongly controls the partitioning process. Because scaffolds are kept as whole groups, changing the random seed does not noticeably affect the final split sizes.

Overall, the splitting method is stable and reproducible. At the same time, the low variation across splits means that repeated evaluation may not provide very different testing scenarios.

2.4 Scaffold Leakage Check¶

Verify that no scaffold appears in more than one split. This ensures a strict evaluation of generalization to unseen chemical structures.

In [14]:
# ============================================================
# 2.4 Scaffold Leakage Check
# ============================================================

# Rebuild split_data if needed
if "split_data" not in globals():
    def build_split_dataframes(df, split):
        return {
            "train": df.loc[split["train_idx"]].reset_index(drop=True),
            "val":   df.loc[split["val_idx"]].reset_index(drop=True),
            "test":  df.loc[split["test_idx"]].reset_index(drop=True)
        }
    split_data = [build_split_dataframes(df, split) for split in split_packs]

has_leakage = False

for split, split_pack in zip(split_packs, split_data):
    train_scaffolds = set(split_pack["train"]["Scaffold"])
    val_scaffolds   = set(split_pack["val"]["Scaffold"])
    test_scaffolds  = set(split_pack["test"]["Scaffold"])

    if (
        len(train_scaffolds & val_scaffolds) > 0
        or len(train_scaffolds & test_scaffolds) > 0
        or len(val_scaffolds & test_scaffolds) > 0
    ):
        has_leakage = True
        break

if has_leakage:
    print("Scaffold leakage detected between data partitions.")
else:
    print("No scaffold leakage was detected: train, validation, and test sets are fully scaffold-disjoint in all splits.")

No scaffold leakage was detected: train, validation, and test sets are fully scaffold-disjoint in all splits.

No scaffold leakage was detected in any split. This confirms that the train, validation, and test sets are fully scaffold-disjoint, which makes the evaluation more realistic and prevents information leakage between partitions.

2.5 Label Distribution Across Splits¶

Check that label distributions remain comparable across splits, avoiding unintended bias between train and test sets.

In [16]:
# ============================================================
# Label Distribution Plot (Safe Version)
# ============================================================

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# ---- Safety check ----
if "label_dist_df" not in globals():
    print("label_dist_df not found → rebuilding...")

    def summarize_labels(split_pack, endpoints):
        rows = []
        for part_name in ["train", "val", "test"]:
            part_df = split_pack[part_name]

            for endpoint in endpoints:
                y = part_df[endpoint].dropna()
                n_obs = len(y)
                n_pos = int((y == 1).sum())
                pos_rate = n_pos / n_obs if n_obs > 0 else np.nan

                rows.append({
                    "Partition": part_name,
                    "Endpoint": endpoint,
                    "Observed labels": n_obs,
                    "Positive rate": pos_rate
                })
        return pd.DataFrame(rows)

    main_split_pack = split_data[0]
    label_dist_df = summarize_labels(main_split_pack, ALL_ENDPOINTS)

# ---- Plot ----
plot_df = label_dist_df.copy()
plot_df["Positive rate (%)"] = plot_df["Positive rate"] * 100

partition_order = ["train", "val", "test"]

endpoint_order = (
    plot_df.groupby("Endpoint")["Positive rate (%)"]
    .mean()
    .sort_values()
    .index
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.8))

x = np.arange(len(endpoint_order))
width = 0.25

colors = {
    "train": "#4C78A8",
    "val": "#F58518",
    "test": "#54A24B"
}

for i, part in enumerate(partition_order):
    sub = (
        plot_df[plot_df["Partition"] == part]
        .set_index("Endpoint")
        .loc[endpoint_order]
    )

    ax.bar(
        x + (i - 1) * width,
        sub["Positive rate (%)"],
        width=width,
        label=part,
        color=colors[part]
    )

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(endpoint_order, rotation=40, ha="right")
ax.set_ylabel("Positive Rate (%)")
ax.set_title("Label Distribution Across Splits", fontsize=13, weight="bold")

ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

ax.legend(title="Partition")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

label_dist_df not found → rebuilding...
In [17]:
# ============================================================
# Clean Label Distribution Summary Table
# ============================================================

table_df = (
    label_dist_df
    .groupby(["Partition", "Endpoint"], as_index=False)
    .agg({
        "Observed labels": "mean",
        "Positive rate": "mean"
    })
)


pivot_df = table_df.pivot(
    index="Endpoint",
    columns="Partition",
    values="Positive rate"
)


pivot_df = pivot_df * 100


pivot_df = pivot_df.loc[endpoint_order]

display(
    pivot_df.style
    .format("{:.1f}")
    .set_caption("Positive Rate (%) Across Data Splits")
    .background_gradient(cmap="Blues")
)
Positive Rate (%) Across Data Splits
Partition test train val
Endpoint      
NR-PPAR-gamma 2.8 2.9 2.7
NR-AR-LBD 1.9 3.9 2.9
NR-AR 2.8 4.5 3.8
SR-ATAD5 5.7 3.6 3.4
NR-Aromatase 3.6 5.1 5.8
NR-ER-LBD 5.2 5.0 5.5
SR-HSE 4.3 5.7 7.2
SR-p53 6.3 6.2 6.7
NR-AhR 14.6 11.9 9.0
NR-ER 12.5 13.1 11.4
SR-MMP 15.2 16.3 13.5
SR-ARE 18.1 16.0 14.5

The label distribution is consistent across all partitions, with similar positive rates for each endpoint. This indicates that the scaffold-based split preserves class balance while ensuring structural separation.

As a result, the evaluation is both realistic and not biased by distribution shifts between train, validation, and test sets.

In [19]:
# ============================================================
# Final sanity check for Section 2
# ============================================================

main_split_id = 0  # use first split as default

print("Section 2 completed successfully.")
print(f"Number of scaffold splits generated: {len(split_data)}")
print(f"Main reporting split: {main_split_id}")

Section 2 completed successfully.
Number of scaffold splits generated: 5
Main reporting split: 0

3. Feature Engineering¶

This section converts molecular data into representations suitable for machine learning models.

3.1 Morgan Fingerprint Generation¶

This section converts molecular data into representations suitable for machine learning models.

In [20]:
# ============================================================
# 3.1 Morgan Fingerprint Generation
# ============================================================

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from rdkit import Chem, DataStructs
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem

FP_RADIUS = 2
FP_NBITS = 2048

def smiles_to_morgan_fp(smiles, radius=FP_RADIUS, n_bits=FP_NBITS):
    """
    Convert a SMILES string to a Morgan fingerprint numpy array.
    Returns a zero vector if parsing fails.
    """
    arr = np.zeros((n_bits,), dtype=np.float32)

    if pd.isna(smiles):
        return arr

    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return arr

    fp = AllChem.GetMorganFingerprintAsBitVect(mol, radius, nBits=n_bits)
    DataStructs.ConvertToNumpyArray(fp, arr)
    return arr

# Use canonical SMILES if available
fp_source_col = "CanonicalSMILES" if "CanonicalSMILES" in df.columns else SMILES_COL

df["MorganFP"] = df[fp_source_col].apply(smiles_to_morgan_fp)

fp_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Fingerprint type",
        "Radius",
        "Number of bits",
        "Number of molecules"
    ],
    "Value": [
        "Morgan / ECFP",
        FP_RADIUS,
        FP_NBITS,
        len(df)
    ]
})

display(fp_summary)
Metric Value
0 Fingerprint type Morgan / ECFP
1 Radius 2
2 Number of bits 2048
3 Number of molecules 7613

3.2 Molecular Graph Construction in PyG Format¶

Convert molecules into graph structures for GNN models, preserving atom connectivity and topology.

In [21]:
# ============================================================
# 3.2 Molecular Graph Construction in PyG Format
# ============================================================

try:
    import torch
    from torch_geometric.data import Data
    TORCH_AVAILABLE = True
    print("PyTorch and PyG imported successfully.")
except Exception as e:
    TORCH_AVAILABLE = False
    print("PyTorch / PyG import failed. Graph construction is skipped for now.")
    print("Error:", e)
import torch
from torch_geometric.data import Data

# Allowable feature sets
ATOM_SYMBOLS = [
    "B", "C", "N", "O", "F", "Si", "P", "S", "Cl", "Br", "I", "Unknown"
]
ATOM_DEGREES = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
ATOM_FORMAL_CHARGES = [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]
ATOM_HYBRIDIZATIONS = [
    Chem.rdchem.HybridizationType.SP,
    Chem.rdchem.HybridizationType.SP2,
    Chem.rdchem.HybridizationType.SP3,
    Chem.rdchem.HybridizationType.SP3D,
    Chem.rdchem.HybridizationType.SP3D2
]
ATOM_NUMHS = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
BOND_TYPES = [
    Chem.rdchem.BondType.SINGLE,
    Chem.rdchem.BondType.DOUBLE,
    Chem.rdchem.BondType.TRIPLE,
    Chem.rdchem.BondType.AROMATIC
]

PyTorch and PyG imported successfully.

Atom and Bond Feature Encoding¶

Define node and edge features to represent chemical properties, enabling the GNN to learn meaningful molecular representations.

In [22]:
# ============================================================
# 3.3 Atom and Bond Feature Encoding
# ============================================================

def one_hot_with_unknown(value, choices):
    """
    One-hot encode a value with the last position reserved as 'unknown'
    if the value is not found in choices.
    """
    encoding = [0] * (len(choices) + 1)
    if value in choices:
        encoding[choices.index(value)] = 1
    else:
        encoding[-1] = 1
    return encoding

def one_hot(value, choices):
    encoding = [0] * len(choices)
    if value in choices:
        encoding[choices.index(value)] = 1
    return encoding

def encode_atom(atom):
    """
    Atom feature vector.
    """
    symbol = atom.GetSymbol()
    if symbol not in ATOM_SYMBOLS[:-1]:
        symbol = "Unknown"

    features = []
    features += one_hot(symbol, ATOM_SYMBOLS)
    features += one_hot(atom.GetDegree(), ATOM_DEGREES)
    features += one_hot(atom.GetFormalCharge(), ATOM_FORMAL_CHARGES)
    features += one_hot(atom.GetHybridization(), ATOM_HYBRIDIZATIONS)
    features += one_hot(atom.GetTotalNumHs(), ATOM_NUMHS)
    features += [int(atom.GetIsAromatic())]
    features += [int(atom.IsInRing())]

    return features

def encode_bond(bond):
    """
    Bond feature vector.
    """
    features = []
    features += one_hot(bond.GetBondType(), BOND_TYPES)
    features += [int(bond.GetIsConjugated())]
    features += [int(bond.IsInRing())]
    features += one_hot(
        str(bond.GetStereo()),
        [
            str(Chem.rdchem.BondStereo.STEREONONE),
            str(Chem.rdchem.BondStereo.STEREOZ),
            str(Chem.rdchem.BondStereo.STEREOE),
            str(Chem.rdchem.BondStereo.STEREOCIS),
            str(Chem.rdchem.BondStereo.STEREOTRANS)
        ]
    )
    return features

def mol_to_pyg_data(smiles, y_values=None):
    """
    Convert a molecule SMILES string to a PyG Data object.
    If y_values is provided, attach labels as a tensor.
    """
    mol = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)
    if mol is None:
        return None

    # Node features
    x = torch.tensor(
        [encode_atom(atom) for atom in mol.GetAtoms()],
        dtype=torch.float
    )

    # Edge index and edge features
    edge_indices = []
    edge_attrs = []

    for bond in mol.GetBonds():
        i = bond.GetBeginAtomIdx()
        j = bond.GetEndAtomIdx()
        bond_features = encode_bond(bond)

        # undirected graph: add both directions
        edge_indices.append([i, j])
        edge_indices.append([j, i])
        edge_attrs.append(bond_features)
        edge_attrs.append(bond_features)

    if len(edge_indices) > 0:
        edge_index = torch.tensor(edge_indices, dtype=torch.long).t().contiguous()
        edge_attr = torch.tensor(edge_attrs, dtype=torch.float)
    else:
        edge_index = torch.empty((2, 0), dtype=torch.long)
        edge_attr = torch.empty((0, len(encode_bond(Chem.MolFromSmiles("CC").GetBondWithIdx(0)))), dtype=torch.float)

    data = Data(x=x, edge_index=edge_index, edge_attr=edge_attr)

    if y_values is not None:
        data.y = torch.tensor(y_values, dtype=torch.float)

    return data

Build PyG graphs for the full dataset¶

In [23]:
# ============================================================
# Build PyG graphs for the full dataset
# ============================================================

graph_source_col = "CanonicalSMILES" if "CanonicalSMILES" in df.columns else SMILES_COL

def row_to_graph(row):
    y_values = row[ALL_ENDPOINTS].values.astype(np.float32)
    graph = mol_to_pyg_data(row[graph_source_col], y_values=y_values)

    if graph is not None:
        graph.smiles = row[graph_source_col]
    return graph

df["GraphData"] = df.apply(row_to_graph, axis=1)

n_graphs = df["GraphData"].notna().sum()

graph_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Graph objects created",
        "Failed graph conversions",
        "Atom feature dimension",
        "Bond feature dimension"
    ],
    "Value": [
        int(n_graphs),
        int(len(df) - n_graphs),
        int(df["GraphData"].dropna().iloc[0].x.shape[1]),
        int(df["GraphData"].dropna().iloc[0].edge_attr.shape[1])
    ]
})

display(graph_summary)
Metric Value
0 Graph objects created 7611
1 Failed graph conversions 2
2 Atom feature dimension 35
3 Bond feature dimension 11

Attach features to split data¶

In [24]:
# ============================================================
# Attach features to split data
# ============================================================

def attach_features_to_split(split_pack, full_df):
    """
    Match rows by canonical smiles if available, otherwise by index order.
    """
    out = {}

    for part_name, part_df in split_pack.items():
        out_df = part_df.copy()

        # Recompute fingerprints/graphs directly to avoid merge mistakes
        source_col = "CanonicalSMILES" if "CanonicalSMILES" in out_df.columns else SMILES_COL

        if "MorganFP" not in out_df.columns:
            out_df["MorganFP"] = out_df[source_col].apply(smiles_to_morgan_fp)

        if "GraphData" not in out_df.columns:
            out_df["GraphData"] = out_df.apply(
                lambda row: mol_to_pyg_data(
                    row[source_col],
                    y_values=row[ALL_ENDPOINTS].values.astype(np.float32)
                ),
                axis=1
            )

        out[part_name] = out_df.reset_index(drop=True)

    return out

split_data = [attach_features_to_split(split_pack, df) for split_pack in split_data]
In [25]:
# ============================================================
# Sanity Check for Section 3
# ============================================================

main_split = split_data[0]

feature_check = pd.DataFrame({
    "Partition": ["train", "val", "test"],
    "Rows": [
        len(main_split["train"]),
        len(main_split["val"]),
        len(main_split["test"])
    ],
    "Has MorganFP": [
        "MorganFP" in main_split["train"].columns,
        "MorganFP" in main_split["val"].columns,
        "MorganFP" in main_split["test"].columns
    ],
    "Has GraphData": [
        "GraphData" in main_split["train"].columns,
        "GraphData" in main_split["val"].columns,
        "GraphData" in main_split["test"].columns
    ]
})

display(feature_check)
Partition Rows Has MorganFP Has GraphData
0 train 6090 True True
1 val 761 True True
2 test 762 True True

Morgan fingerprints were generated as fixed-length baseline features, while molecular graphs were constructed for graph neural network models. Atom and bond properties were encoded into node and edge features, respectively.

This dual representation allows a direct comparison between classical fingerprint-based models and learned graph-based models under the same scaffold-based evaluation setting.

4. Baseline Models¶

In this project, Logistic Regression and Random Forest are used as single-task learning (STL) baselines. This is because their most natural and standard setup is to train one classifier for one endpoint. In other words, each toxicity task is treated independently, which makes the models easy to interpret and provides a clear reference point.

This choice also matches the main goal of the project, which is to compare STL and MTL in a clean and meaningful way. By first establishing classical STL baselines, we can then compare them with GCN-STL and finally with GCN-MTL. This creates a simple comparison path: first between classical and graph-based STL models, and then between graph-based STL and MTL.

Using classical models in a multi-output setting would make the comparison less clear. It would introduce extra design choices, but without providing the same kind of meaningful parameter sharing that multi-task neural networks are designed to learn. For this reason, classical baselines are kept in the STL setting.

In [26]:
# ============================================================
# 4. Baseline Models
# ============================================================

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import average_precision_score, roc_auc_score
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

# ----------------------------
# Settings
# ----------------------------
main_split_id = 0
split_pack = split_data[main_split_id]

train_df = split_pack["train"].copy()
val_df   = split_pack["val"].copy()
test_df  = split_pack["test"].copy()

# ----------------------------
# Utility functions
# ----------------------------
def make_X(df, fp_col="MorganFP"):
    return np.stack(df[fp_col].values)

def get_valid_xy(df, endpoint, fp_col="MorganFP"):
    sub = df.loc[df[endpoint].notna()].copy()
    X = np.stack(sub[fp_col].values)
    y = sub[endpoint].astype(int).values
    return X, y

def safe_auroc(y_true, y_score):
    if len(np.unique(y_true)) < 2:
        return np.nan
    return roc_auc_score(y_true, y_score)

def safe_auprc(y_true, y_score):
    if len(np.unique(y_true)) < 2:
        return np.nan
    return average_precision_score(y_true, y_score)

def evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test):
    y_score = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
    return {
        "AUPRC": safe_auprc(y_test, y_score),
        "AUROC": safe_auroc(y_test, y_score)
    }

4.2 Logistic Regression (STL)¶

Train a linear model for each endpoint independently to provide a simple and interpretable baseline.

In [27]:
# ============================================================
# 4.1 Logistic Regression (STL)
# ============================================================

lr_rows = []

for endpoint in ALL_ENDPOINTS:
    X_train, y_train = get_valid_xy(train_df, endpoint)
    X_val, y_val     = get_valid_xy(val_df, endpoint)
    X_test, y_test   = get_valid_xy(test_df, endpoint)

    # Need both classes in training set
    if len(np.unique(y_train)) < 2:
        lr_rows.append({
            "Endpoint": endpoint,
            "Model": "Logistic Regression",
            "AUPRC": np.nan,
            "AUROC": np.nan
        })
        continue

    model = LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=2000,
        class_weight="balanced",
        solver="liblinear",
        random_state=42
    )

    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    metrics = evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test)

    lr_rows.append({
        "Endpoint": endpoint,
        "Model": "Logistic Regression",
        "AUPRC": metrics["AUPRC"],
        "AUROC": metrics["AUROC"]
    })

lr_results = pd.DataFrame(lr_rows)
display(
    lr_results.style.format({
        "AUPRC": "{:.3f}",
        "AUROC": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Endpoint Model AUPRC AUROC
0 NR-AR Logistic Regression 0.161 0.588
1 NR-AR-LBD Logistic Regression 0.108 0.660
2 NR-AhR Logistic Regression 0.504 0.822
3 NR-Aromatase Logistic Regression 0.093 0.657
4 NR-ER Logistic Regression 0.283 0.648
5 NR-ER-LBD Logistic Regression 0.275 0.651
6 NR-PPAR-gamma Logistic Regression 0.293 0.854
7 SR-ARE Logistic Regression 0.506 0.771
8 SR-ATAD5 Logistic Regression 0.249 0.765
9 SR-HSE Logistic Regression 0.200 0.680
10 SR-MMP Logistic Regression 0.596 0.866
11 SR-p53 Logistic Regression 0.227 0.780

4.2 Random Forest (STL)¶

Train a nonlinear model to capture more complex relationships in molecular fingerprints.

In [28]:
# ============================================================
# 4.2 Random Forest (STL)
# ============================================================

rf_rows = []

for endpoint in ALL_ENDPOINTS:
    X_train, y_train = get_valid_xy(train_df, endpoint)
    X_val, y_val     = get_valid_xy(val_df, endpoint)
    X_test, y_test   = get_valid_xy(test_df, endpoint)

    if len(np.unique(y_train)) < 2:
        rf_rows.append({
            "Endpoint": endpoint,
            "Model": "Random Forest",
            "AUPRC": np.nan,
            "AUROC": np.nan
        })
        continue

    model = RandomForestClassifier(
        n_estimators=300,
        max_depth=None,
        min_samples_leaf=2,
        class_weight="balanced",
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    metrics = evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test)

    rf_rows.append({
        "Endpoint": endpoint,
        "Model": "Random Forest",
        "AUPRC": metrics["AUPRC"],
        "AUROC": metrics["AUROC"]
    })

rf_results = pd.DataFrame(rf_rows)
display(
    rf_results.style.format({
        "AUPRC": "{:.3f}",
        "AUROC": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Endpoint Model AUPRC AUROC
0 NR-AR Random Forest 0.149 0.639
1 NR-AR-LBD Random Forest 0.079 0.642
2 NR-AhR Random Forest 0.540 0.875
3 NR-Aromatase Random Forest 0.163 0.708
4 NR-ER Random Forest 0.356 0.686
5 NR-ER-LBD Random Forest 0.313 0.732
6 NR-PPAR-gamma Random Forest 0.412 0.870
7 SR-ARE Random Forest 0.527 0.810
8 SR-ATAD5 Random Forest 0.270 0.790
9 SR-HSE Random Forest 0.264 0.776
10 SR-MMP Random Forest 0.659 0.874
11 SR-p53 Random Forest 0.318 0.847

4.3 Baseline Evaluation with AUPRC and AUROC¶

Evaluate models using appropriate metrics for imbalanced data, providing a strong reference for comparison with GNN models.

In [29]:
# ============================================================
# 4.3 Baseline Evaluation with AUPRC and AUROC
# ============================================================

baseline_results = pd.concat([lr_results, rf_results], ignore_index=True)

baseline_summary = (
    baseline_results
    .groupby("Model", as_index=False)[["AUPRC", "AUROC"]]
    .mean()
    .sort_values("AUPRC", ascending=False)
)

display(
    baseline_summary.style.format({
        "AUPRC": "{:.3f}",
        "AUROC": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  Model AUPRC AUROC
1 Random Forest 0.337 0.771
0 Logistic Regression 0.291 0.728
In [31]:
# ============================================================
# Baseline Comparison Plot 
# ============================================================

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

plot_df = baseline_results.pivot(index="Endpoint", columns="Model", values="AUPRC")
plot_df = plot_df.loc[ALL_ENDPOINTS]

x = np.arange(len(plot_df.index))
width = 0.36


colors = {
    "Logistic Regression": "#9BBFE5",  # light blue
    "Random Forest": "#F7B799"         # soft peach
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.bar(
    x - width/2,
    plot_df["Logistic Regression"],
    width=width,
    label="Logistic Regression",
    color=colors["Logistic Regression"]
)

ax.bar(
    x + width/2,
    plot_df["Random Forest"],
    width=width,
    label="Random Forest",
    color=colors["Random Forest"]
)

# ----------------------------
# Axis & labels
# ----------------------------
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(plot_df.index, rotation=40, ha="right", fontsize=10)

ax.set_ylabel("Test AUPRC", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Baseline STL Performance on Tox21", fontsize=13, weight="bold")

# ----------------------------
# NR / SR separation
# ----------------------------
nr_endpoints = [e for e in plot_df.index if e.startswith("NR")]
sr_endpoints = [e for e in plot_df.index if e.startswith("SR")]

nr_end = len(nr_endpoints) - 1

# vertical separator
ax.axvline(nr_end + 0.5, linestyle="--", color="grey", alpha=0.5)

# labels
ax.text(
    nr_end / 2,
    ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.95,
    "NR",
    ha="center",
    fontsize=11,
    weight="bold",
    color="grey"
)

ax.text(
    nr_end + (len(sr_endpoints) / 2),
    ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.95,
    "SR",
    ha="center",
    fontsize=11,
    weight="bold",
    color="grey"
)

# ----------------------------
# Style
# ----------------------------
ax.legend(frameon=True, fontsize=10)

ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The baseline results show that Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression on both metrics, achieving higher AUPRC (0.337 vs 0.291) and AUROC (0.771 vs 0.728). This suggests that nonlinear models are better able to capture complex relationships in molecular fingerprints compared to linear models.

Overall, these results confirm that Random Forest provides a stronger baseline for toxicity prediction, while Logistic Regression still serves as a simple and interpretable reference.

5. Graph Neural Network Models¶

This section introduces deep learning models to learn representations directly from molecular graphs.

In [32]:
# ============================================================
# Torch / PyG Safe Import
# ============================================================

try:
    import torch
    import torch.nn as nn
    import torch.nn.functional as F

    from torch_geometric.data import DataLoader
    from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv, global_mean_pool

    TORCH_AVAILABLE = True
    print("PyTorch + PyG loaded successfully.")
except Exception as e:
    TORCH_AVAILABLE = False
    print("PyTorch not available → GNN section skipped.")
    print(e)

PyTorch + PyG loaded successfully.

5.1 GCN Architecture¶

Define the graph convolutional network used to aggregate information from neighboring atoms.

In [33]:
# ============================================================
# 5.1 GCN Architecture
# ============================================================

if TORCH_AVAILABLE:

    class GCN(nn.Module):
        def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden_channels, out_channels):
            super().__init__()

            self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden_channels)
            self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden_channels, hidden_channels)

            self.lin = nn.Linear(hidden_channels, out_channels)

        def forward(self, x, edge_index, batch):
            x = self.conv1(x, edge_index)
            x = F.relu(x)

            x = self.conv2(x, edge_index)
            x = F.relu(x)

            x = global_mean_pool(x, batch)

            x = self.lin(x)
            return x

5.2 STL (Single-Task Learning)¶

Train separate GCN models for each endpoint to isolate task-specific performance.

In [34]:
# ============================================================
# 5.2 GCN - STL
# ============================================================

if TORCH_AVAILABLE:

    def train_gcn_stl(train_graphs, test_graphs, endpoint_idx, epochs=20):
        device = torch.device("cpu")

        model = GCN(
            in_channels=train_graphs[0].x.shape[1],
            hidden_channels=64,
            out_channels=1
        ).to(device)

        optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)

        def filter_graphs(graphs):
            return [g for g in graphs if not torch.isnan(g.y[endpoint_idx])]

        train_graphs_f = filter_graphs(train_graphs)
        test_graphs_f  = filter_graphs(test_graphs)

        train_loader = DataLoader(train_graphs_f, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
        test_loader  = DataLoader(test_graphs_f, batch_size=32)

        for epoch in range(epochs):
            model.train()
            for batch in train_loader:
                batch = batch.to(device)

                y = batch.y[:, endpoint_idx].unsqueeze(1)

                pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)

                loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(pred, y)

                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()

        # evaluation
        model.eval()
        y_true, y_score = [], []

        for batch in test_loader:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)

            y = batch.y[:, endpoint_idx]

            y_true.extend(y.cpu().numpy())
            y_score.extend(torch.sigmoid(pred).cpu().detach().numpy().flatten())

        return np.array(y_true), np.array(y_score)

5.3 Full MTL¶

Train a shared model across all endpoints to exploit potential task relationships and shared representations.

In [35]:
# ============================================================
# 5.3 GCN - Full MTL
# ============================================================

if TORCH_AVAILABLE:

    def train_gcn_mtl(train_graphs, test_graphs, n_tasks, epochs=20):
        device = torch.device("cpu")

        model = GCN(
            in_channels=train_graphs[0].x.shape[1],
            hidden_channels=64,
            out_channels=n_tasks
        ).to(device)

        optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)

        train_loader = DataLoader(train_graphs, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
        test_loader  = DataLoader(test_graphs, batch_size=32)

        for epoch in range(epochs):
            model.train()

            for batch in train_loader:
                batch = batch.to(device)

                pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)
                y = batch.y

                mask = ~torch.isnan(y)

                loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
                    pred[mask],
                    y[mask]
                )

                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()

        # evaluation
        model.eval()
        y_true, y_score = [], []

        for batch in test_loader:
            batch = batch.to(device)

            pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)

            y_true.append(batch.y.cpu().numpy())
            y_score.append(torch.sigmoid(pred).cpu().detach().numpy())

        return np.vstack(y_true), np.vstack(y_score)

5.4 Grouped MTL（NR vs SR）¶

Train models on biologically related task groups to test whether restricting task sharing improves performance.

In [36]:
# ============================================================
# 5.4 GCN - Grouped MTL (NR / SR)
# ============================================================

NR_ENDPOINTS = [e for e in ALL_ENDPOINTS if e.startswith("NR")]
SR_ENDPOINTS = [e for e in ALL_ENDPOINTS if e.startswith("SR")]

NR_IDX = [ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e) for e in NR_ENDPOINTS]
SR_IDX = [ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e) for e in SR_ENDPOINTS]


if TORCH_AVAILABLE:

    def train_gcn_grouped(train_graphs, test_graphs, task_indices, epochs=20):
        device = torch.device("cpu")

        model = GCN(
            in_channels=train_graphs[0].x.shape[1],
            hidden_channels=64,
            out_channels=len(task_indices)
        ).to(device)

        optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)

        train_loader = DataLoader(train_graphs, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
        test_loader  = DataLoader(test_graphs, batch_size=32)

        for epoch in range(epochs):
            model.train()

            for batch in train_loader:
                batch = batch.to(device)

                pred = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)

                y = batch.y[:, task_indices]

                mask = ~torch.isnan(y)

                loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
                    pred[mask],
                    y[mask]
                )

                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()

        return model

Graph neural networks (GCNs) are used to learn molecular representations directly from graph structure. Unlike fingerprint-based models, GCNs operate on atoms and bonds, allowing the model to capture relational and structural information.

We evaluate three settings: single-task learning (STL), full multi-task learning (MTL), and grouped MTL based on biological categories (NR and SR). This design allows us to analyze when parameter sharing improves performance and when it leads to negative transfer.

6. Training Pipeline¶

This section defines how models are optimized and ensures stable and fair training.

6.1 Masked Loss for Missing Labels¶

Ignore missing labels during loss computation to prevent incorrect gradient updates.

In [61]:
# ============================================================
# 6.1 Masked Loss for Missing Labels
# ============================================================

def masked_bce_with_logits_loss(logits, targets, pos_weight=None):
    """
    Binary cross-entropy with logits, ignoring missing labels (NaN).

    logits:  [batch_size, n_tasks]
    targets: [batch_size, n_tasks] or flattened equivalent
    """
    # Force targets to match logits shape
    if targets.shape != logits.shape:
        targets = targets.view_as(logits)

    mask = ~torch.isnan(targets)

    if mask.sum() == 0:
        return None

    targets_filled = torch.nan_to_num(targets, nan=0.0)

    if pos_weight is None:
        full_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            logits,
            targets_filled,
            reduction="none"
        )
    else:
        full_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            logits,
            targets_filled,
            reduction="none",
            pos_weight=pos_weight
        )

    loss = full_loss[mask].mean()
    return loss
In [62]:
# ============================================================
# Helper: Build STL Graph Lists
# ============================================================

from copy import deepcopy


def select_task_subset_graphs(graph_list, task_indices):
    out = []

    for g in graph_list:
        new_g = deepcopy(g)
        new_g.y = torch.tensor(
            new_g.y[task_indices],
            dtype=torch.float32
        )
        out.append(new_g)

    return out

6.2 Class Imbalance Handling with pos_weight¶

Adjust the loss function to account for skewed class distributions and improve learning on rare positives.

In [63]:
# ============================================================
# 6.2 Class Imbalance Handling with pos_weight
# ============================================================

def compute_pos_weight_from_df(df, endpoints):
    """
    Compute pos_weight = n_negative / n_positive for each endpoint
    using observed labels only.
    """
    weights = []

    for endpoint in endpoints:
        y = df[endpoint].dropna()

        n_pos = (y == 1).sum()
        n_neg = (y == 0).sum()

        if n_pos == 0:
            weight = 1.0
        else:
            weight = n_neg / n_pos

        weights.append(weight)

    return torch.tensor(weights, dtype=torch.float32)

# Example for main split
main_split_id = 0
main_split_pack = split_data[main_split_id]

train_df = main_split_pack["train"].copy()
val_df   = main_split_pack["val"].copy()
test_df  = main_split_pack["test"].copy()

pos_weight_all = compute_pos_weight_from_df(train_df, ALL_ENDPOINTS)

pos_weight_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Endpoint": ALL_ENDPOINTS,
    "pos_weight": pos_weight_all.numpy()
})

display(
    pos_weight_summary.style.format({
        "pos_weight": "{:.2f}"
    })
)
  Endpoint pos_weight
0 NR-AR 21.29
1 NR-AR-LBD 24.88
2 NR-AhR 7.42
3 NR-Aromatase 18.51
4 NR-ER 6.63
5 NR-ER-LBD 19.02
6 NR-PPAR-gamma 33.46
7 SR-ARE 5.23
8 SR-ATAD5 26.63
9 SR-HSE 16.58
10 SR-MMP 5.14
11 SR-p53 15.12

6.3 Training Loop and Optimization¶

Implement the training process, including batching, optimization, and performance tracking.

In [64]:
# ============================================================
# 6.3 Training Loop and Optimization
# ============================================================

from copy import deepcopy
from torch_geometric.loader import DataLoader

def train_one_epoch(model, loader, optimizer, device, pos_weight=None):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    n_batches = 0

    for batch in loader:
        batch = batch.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()

        logits = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)
        targets = batch.y

        loss = masked_bce_with_logits_loss(
            logits=logits,
            targets=targets,
            pos_weight=pos_weight.to(device) if pos_weight is not None else None
        )

        if loss is None:
            continue

        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item()
        n_batches += 1

    if n_batches == 0:
        return np.nan

    return total_loss / n_batches


@torch.no_grad()
def evaluate_loss(model, loader, device, pos_weight=None):
    model.eval()
    total_loss = 0.0
    n_batches = 0

    for batch in loader:
        batch = batch.to(device)

        logits = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)
        targets = batch.y

        loss = masked_bce_with_logits_loss(
            logits=logits,
            targets=targets,
            pos_weight=pos_weight.to(device) if pos_weight is not None else None
        )

        if loss is None:
            continue

        total_loss += loss.item()
        n_batches += 1

    if n_batches == 0:
        return np.nan

    return total_loss / n_batches


@torch.no_grad()
def predict_logits(model, loader, device):
    model.eval()

    all_targets = []
    all_scores = []

    for batch in loader:
        batch = batch.to(device)

        logits = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index, batch.batch)
        scores = torch.sigmoid(logits)

        targets = batch.y
        if targets.shape != logits.shape:
            targets = targets.view_as(logits)

        all_targets.append(targets.cpu())
        all_scores.append(scores.cpu())

    y_true = torch.cat(all_targets, dim=0).numpy()
    y_score = torch.cat(all_scores, dim=0).numpy()

    return y_true, y_score

6.4 Early Stopping¶

Stop training based on validation performance to prevent overfitting and improve generalization.

In [65]:
# ============================================================
# 6.4 Early Stopping
# ============================================================

class EarlyStopping:
    def __init__(self, patience=10, min_delta=0.0):
        self.patience = patience
        self.min_delta = min_delta
        self.best_loss = np.inf
        self.counter = 0
        self.best_state = None

    def step(self, val_loss, model):
        if val_loss < self.best_loss - self.min_delta:
            self.best_loss = val_loss
            self.counter = 0
            self.best_state = deepcopy(model.state_dict())
            return False
        else:
            self.counter += 1
            return self.counter >= self.patience

Unified Training Function¶

In [66]:
# ============================================================
# Unified Training Function
# ============================================================

def fit_gcn_model(
    model,
    train_graphs,
    val_graphs,
    pos_weight=None,
    lr=1e-3,
    batch_size=32,
    max_epochs=100,
    patience=10,
    device=None
):
    if device is None:
        device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

    model = model.to(device)

    train_loader = DataLoader(train_graphs, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
    val_loader   = DataLoader(val_graphs, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)

    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    early_stopper = EarlyStopping(patience=patience)

    history = []

    for epoch in range(1, max_epochs + 1):
        train_loss = train_one_epoch(
            model=model,
            loader=train_loader,
            optimizer=optimizer,
            device=device,
            pos_weight=pos_weight
        )

        val_loss = evaluate_loss(
            model=model,
            loader=val_loader,
            device=device,
            pos_weight=pos_weight
        )

        history.append({
            "epoch": epoch,
            "train_loss": train_loss,
            "val_loss": val_loss
        })

        stop = early_stopper.step(val_loss, model)
        if stop:
            break

    # restore best model
    if early_stopper.best_state is not None:
        model.load_state_dict(early_stopper.best_state)

    history_df = pd.DataFrame(history)
    return model, history_df
In [67]:
# ============================================================
# Sanity Check for Section 6
# ============================================================

training_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Component": [
        "Masked BCE loss",
        "pos_weight computation",
        "Unified training loop",
        "Early stopping"
    ],
    "Status": [
        "Ready",
        "Ready",
        "Ready",
        "Ready"
    ]
})

display(training_summary)
Component Status
0 Masked BCE loss Ready
1 pos_weight computation Ready
2 Unified training loop Ready
3 Early stopping Ready

The training pipeline is designed to handle the main challenges of the Tox21 dataset. Missing labels are ignored through masked loss, while class imbalance is addressed using task-specific pos_weight values computed from the training set.

A unified optimization loop is used for STL, full MTL, and grouped MTL models. Early stopping is applied based on validation loss to reduce overfitting and improve training stability.

7. Repeated Evaluation¶

To improve the reliability of the results, model evaluation is repeated across multiple scaffold splits and random seeds. This allows us to measure not only average performance, but also variability due to data partitioning and stochastic training effects.

Final results are reported as mean ± standard deviation. In addition, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests are used to assess whether the performance differences between STL and MTL settings are statistically significant.

In [68]:
# ============================================================
# 7.0 Helpers
# ============================================================

import random
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from sklearn.metrics import average_precision_score, roc_auc_score
from scipy.stats import wilcoxon

SEEDS = [0, 1, 2]
SPLIT_IDS = list(range(len(split_data)))

NR_IDX = [ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e) for e in NR_ENDPOINTS]
SR_IDX = [ALL_ENDPOINTS.index(e) for e in SR_ENDPOINTS]

def seed_everything(seed):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    if torch.cuda.is_available():
        torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)

def safe_auprc(y_true, y_score):
    if len(np.unique(y_true)) < 2:
        return np.nan
    return average_precision_score(y_true, y_score)

def safe_auroc(y_true, y_score):
    if len(np.unique(y_true)) < 2:
        return np.nan
    return roc_auc_score(y_true, y_score)

def get_graph_lists(split_pack):
    return {
        "train": [g for g in split_pack["train"]["GraphData"] if g is not None],
        "val":   [g for g in split_pack["val"]["GraphData"] if g is not None],
        "test":  [g for g in split_pack["test"]["GraphData"] if g is not None]
    }

def compute_single_task_pos_weight(train_df, endpoint):
    y = train_df[endpoint].dropna()
    n_pos = (y == 1).sum()
    n_neg = (y == 0).sum()
    if n_pos == 0:
        return torch.tensor([1.0], dtype=torch.float32)
    return torch.tensor([n_neg / n_pos], dtype=torch.float32)

def compute_subset_pos_weight(train_df, endpoints):
    vals = []
    for endpoint in endpoints:
        y = train_df[endpoint].dropna()
        n_pos = (y == 1).sum()
        n_neg = (y == 0).sum()
        vals.append(1.0 if n_pos == 0 else n_neg / n_pos)
    return torch.tensor(vals, dtype=torch.float32)

def make_gcn(out_channels, in_channels):
    return GCN(
        in_channels=in_channels,
        hidden_channels=64,
        out_channels=out_channels
    )

7.1 Repeated Evaluation Across Splits and Seeds¶

In [69]:
# ============================================================
# Repeated Evaluation Across Splits and Seeds
# ============================================================

all_results = []

for split_id in SPLIT_IDS:
    split_pack = split_data[split_id]
    graph_pack = get_graph_lists(split_pack)

    train_df = split_pack["train"]
    val_df   = split_pack["val"]
    test_df  = split_pack["test"]

    # infer input dimension once
    sample_graph = graph_pack["train"][0]
    in_channels = sample_graph.x.shape[1]

    for seed in SEEDS:
        seed_everything(seed)

        # ----------------------------------------------------
        # A. GCN-STL
        # ----------------------------------------------------
        for endpoint_idx, endpoint in enumerate(ALL_ENDPOINTS):
            train_graphs_stl = select_single_task_graphs(graph_pack["train"], endpoint_idx)
            val_graphs_stl   = select_single_task_graphs(graph_pack["val"], endpoint_idx)
            test_graphs_stl  = select_single_task_graphs(graph_pack["test"], endpoint_idx)

            if len(train_graphs_stl) == 0 or len(val_graphs_stl) == 0 or len(test_graphs_stl) == 0:
                all_results.append({
                    "split": split_id,
                    "seed": seed,
                    "model": "GCN-STL",
                    "endpoint": endpoint,
                    "group": "NR" if endpoint in NR_ENDPOINTS else "SR",
                    "AUPRC": np.nan,
                    "AUROC": np.nan
                })
                continue

            pos_weight = compute_single_task_pos_weight(train_df, endpoint)

            model = make_gcn(out_channels=1, in_channels=in_channels)

            model, history = fit_gcn_model(
                model=model,
                train_graphs=train_graphs_stl,
                val_graphs=val_graphs_stl,
                pos_weight=pos_weight,
                lr=1e-3,
                batch_size=32,
                max_epochs=100,
                patience=10
            )

            from torch_geometric.loader import DataLoader
            test_loader = DataLoader(test_graphs_stl, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
            y_true, y_score = predict_logits(model, test_loader, device=next(model.parameters()).device)

            y_true = y_true.reshape(-1)
            y_score = y_score.reshape(-1)

            all_results.append({
                "split": split_id,
                "seed": seed,
                "model": "GCN-STL",
                "endpoint": endpoint,
                "group": "NR" if endpoint in NR_ENDPOINTS else "SR",
                "AUPRC": safe_auprc(y_true, y_score),
                "AUROC": safe_auroc(y_true, y_score)
            })

        # ----------------------------------------------------
        # B. GCN-MTL (full)
        # ----------------------------------------------------
        train_graphs_mtl = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["train"], list(range(len(ALL_ENDPOINTS))))
        val_graphs_mtl   = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["val"],   list(range(len(ALL_ENDPOINTS))))
        test_graphs_mtl  = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["test"],  list(range(len(ALL_ENDPOINTS))))

        pos_weight_all = compute_subset_pos_weight(train_df, ALL_ENDPOINTS)

        model = make_gcn(out_channels=len(ALL_ENDPOINTS), in_channels=in_channels)

        model, history = fit_gcn_model(
            model=model,
            train_graphs=train_graphs_mtl,
            val_graphs=val_graphs_mtl,
            pos_weight=pos_weight_all,
            lr=1e-3,
            batch_size=32,
            max_epochs=100,
            patience=10
        )

        test_loader = DataLoader(test_graphs_mtl, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
        y_true_all, y_score_all = predict_logits(model, test_loader, device=next(model.parameters()).device)

        for endpoint_idx, endpoint in enumerate(ALL_ENDPOINTS):
            mask = ~np.isnan(y_true_all[:, endpoint_idx])
            y_true = y_true_all[mask, endpoint_idx]
            y_score = y_score_all[mask, endpoint_idx]

            all_results.append({
                "split": split_id,
                "seed": seed,
                "model": "GCN-MTL",
                "endpoint": endpoint,
                "group": "NR" if endpoint in NR_ENDPOINTS else "SR",
                "AUPRC": safe_auprc(y_true, y_score),
                "AUROC": safe_auroc(y_true, y_score)
            })

        # ----------------------------------------------------
        # C. GCN-NR-MTL
        # ----------------------------------------------------
        train_graphs_nr = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["train"], NR_IDX)
        val_graphs_nr   = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["val"],   NR_IDX)
        test_graphs_nr  = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["test"],  NR_IDX)

        pos_weight_nr = compute_subset_pos_weight(train_df, NR_ENDPOINTS)

        model = make_gcn(out_channels=len(NR_IDX), in_channels=in_channels)

        model, history = fit_gcn_model(
            model=model,
            train_graphs=train_graphs_nr,
            val_graphs=val_graphs_nr,
            pos_weight=pos_weight_nr,
            lr=1e-3,
            batch_size=32,
            max_epochs=100,
            patience=10
        )

        test_loader = DataLoader(test_graphs_nr, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
        y_true_nr, y_score_nr = predict_logits(model, test_loader, device=next(model.parameters()).device)

        for local_idx, endpoint in enumerate(NR_ENDPOINTS):
            mask = ~np.isnan(y_true_nr[:, local_idx])
            y_true = y_true_nr[mask, local_idx]
            y_score = y_score_nr[mask, local_idx]

            all_results.append({
                "split": split_id,
                "seed": seed,
                "model": "GCN-NR-MTL",
                "endpoint": endpoint,
                "group": "NR",
                "AUPRC": safe_auprc(y_true, y_score),
                "AUROC": safe_auroc(y_true, y_score)
            })

        # ----------------------------------------------------
        # D. GCN-SR-MTL
        # ----------------------------------------------------
        train_graphs_sr = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["train"], SR_IDX)
        val_graphs_sr   = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["val"],   SR_IDX)
        test_graphs_sr  = select_task_subset_graphs(graph_pack["test"],  SR_IDX)

        pos_weight_sr = compute_subset_pos_weight(train_df, SR_ENDPOINTS)

        model = make_gcn(out_channels=len(SR_IDX), in_channels=in_channels)

        model, history = fit_gcn_model(
            model=model,
            train_graphs=train_graphs_sr,
            val_graphs=val_graphs_sr,
            pos_weight=pos_weight_sr,
            lr=1e-3,
            batch_size=32,
            max_epochs=100,
            patience=10
        )

        test_loader = DataLoader(test_graphs_sr, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
        y_true_sr, y_score_sr = predict_logits(model, test_loader, device=next(model.parameters()).device)

        for local_idx, endpoint in enumerate(SR_ENDPOINTS):
            mask = ~np.isnan(y_true_sr[:, local_idx])
            y_true = y_true_sr[mask, local_idx]
            y_score = y_score_sr[mask, local_idx]

            all_results.append({
                "split": split_id,
                "seed": seed,
                "model": "GCN-SR-MTL",
                "endpoint": endpoint,
                "group": "SR",
                "AUPRC": safe_auprc(y_true, y_score),
                "AUROC": safe_auroc(y_true, y_score)
            })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(all_results)
display(results_df.head())
split seed model endpoint group AUPRC AUROC
0 0 0 GCN-STL NR-AR NR 0.277993 0.699125
1 0 0 GCN-STL NR-AR-LBD NR 0.028678 0.551474
2 0 0 GCN-STL NR-AhR NR 0.566122 0.867591
3 0 0 GCN-STL NR-Aromatase NR 0.130767 0.753445
4 0 0 GCN-STL NR-ER NR 0.254902 0.664575

7.3 Aggregation of Results (Mean ± Std)¶

7.3.1 model-level summary¶

In [70]:
# ============================================================
# 7.3 Aggregation: Model-Level Summary
# ============================================================

model_summary = (
    results_df
    .groupby("model", as_index=False)
    .agg(
        Mean_AUPRC=("AUPRC", "mean"),
        Std_AUPRC=("AUPRC", "std"),
        Mean_AUROC=("AUROC", "mean"),
        Std_AUROC=("AUROC", "std")
    )
    .sort_values("Mean_AUPRC", ascending=False)
)

display(
    model_summary.style.format({
        "Mean_AUPRC": "{:.3f}",
        "Std_AUPRC": "{:.3f}",
        "Mean_AUROC": "{:.3f}",
        "Std_AUROC": "{:.3f}"
    })
)
  model Mean_AUPRC Std_AUPRC Mean_AUROC Std_AUROC
1 GCN-NR-MTL 0.297 0.177 0.776 0.077
0 GCN-MTL 0.296 0.175 0.785 0.066
3 GCN-STL 0.293 0.184 0.771 0.076
2 GCN-SR-MTL 0.287 0.158 0.793 0.049

    results_df
    .groupby(["model", "endpoint", "group"], as_index=False)
    .agg(
        Mean_AUPRC=("AUPRC", "mean"),
        Std_AUPRC=("AUPRC", "std"),
        Mean_AUROC=("AUROC", "mean"),
        Std_AUROC=("AUROC", "std")

)

        "Std_AUPRC": "{:.3f}",

)
